arbitrated.notes: Labor tightness is the only driver in front of me, and it has softened relative to last meeting's read. The unemployment rate actually ticked down 0.10pp (4.50%→4.40%), which is directionally against my prior +0.55 "up" call, and the analyst itself downgraded conviction to +0.30 while flagging this as a genuine surprise, not noise they're waving away. That said, the underlying trend metrics (12m change, 3m change, Sahm gap at 0.43pp, distance above natural rate) all still point to a labor market that is loose-and-drifting rather than tight-and-tightening, and Fed communication has shifted toward acknowledging downside employment risk. This is a genuine partial reversal, not a repeat of last month's evidence — the single-month print undercuts the strength of my prior call even though the multi-month trend argument survives. Given my own stated flip condition (a print at/below 4.30% or a hot inflation print) was not fully met — we got close to stabilization but didn't cross it — I don't think this warrants a full reversal of my policy-easing lean, but it does warrant trimming conviction. I'll shade my "up" conviction down from +0.55 to +0.20: still leaning toward the unemployment rate drifting up (looser labor, less tightening priced) but with much less confidence given the surprise print and reduced analyst conviction. Correspondingly I'll trim the front-end short position rather than hold it unchanged or reverse it — the reaction-function case for pricing out tightening is weaker than last month but not gone.
